This is a ninety-one year old female who presented with three months history of episodic abdominal pain mostly in the lower abdomen with on and off presence of right groin bulge.

This video demonstrates robotic assisted transabdominal preperitoneal bilateral obturator and right femoral and inguinal hernia repaired with mesh.
The robotic-assisted procedure involves transabdominal preperitoneal repair of bilateral obturator and right femoral and inguinal hernias using mesh.
A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum, extending from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament.
The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum, starting from the anterior superior iliac spine and extending towards the medial umbilical ligament.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, preparing the area for hernia repair.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, preparing the area for hernia repair.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the symphysis pubis and Cooper's ligament, revealing anatomical landmarks crucial for hernia repair.
The surgeon performs dissection towards the symphysis pubis and Cooper's ligament, advancing the surgical field in the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the inguinal and femoral hernia on the right side, then shifts focus to the right incarcerated obturator hernia, addressing the preperitoneal fat involved.

The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the right inguinal and femoral hernia, then shifts focus to the right incarcerated obturator hernia, addressing the preperitoneal fat in that area.
The surgeon continues dissection to reduce the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the right obturator perineal defect.
The surgeon continues dissection to reduce the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the right obturator perineal defect.
The surgeon continues dissection medially towards the symphysis pubis, focusing on the anatomical structures in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially towards the symphysis pubis, focusing on the anatomical structures in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon prepares to place a three-dimensional mesh into the preperitoneal space, which has been exposed and prepared for hernia repair.
The surgeon prepares to place a three-dimensional mesh within the preperitoneal space, following the creation of a preperitoneal flap.
A large mesh is inserted into the surgical space, and dissections are performed laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly to create adequate space for the mesh placement.
A large mesh is inserted into the surgical space, and dissection is carried out laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly to create adequate space for its placement.
The surgeon makes an incision on the left side and enters the preperitoneal space, mirroring the approach previously performed on the right side.
The surgeon makes an incision on the left side and enters the preperitoneal space, mirroring the previous approach on the right side.
The surgeon continues dissection in the preperitoneal space, extending laterally, superiorly, inferiorly, and medially towards the symphysis pubis to further prepare the area for mesh placement.
The surgeon continues dissection laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly, as well as medially towards the symphysis pubis, expanding the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat that is incarcerated into the left obturator hernia, visibly freeing the hernia from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat that was incarcerated into the left obturator hernia, visibly freeing the hernia from the surrounding tissue.
A large, three-dimensional mesh is inserted into the preperitoneal space, following the creation of adequate space through previous dissections.
The surgeon inserts a large, three-dimensional mesh into the preperitoneal space, expanding the area previously dissected to accommodate the mesh.
The surgeon places the large three-dimensional mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring it covers all visible hernia defects.
The surgeon places the mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring it covers all visible defects.
The surgeon uses a T-seal device to secure the mesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring it remains in place over the hernia defects.
The surgeon uses a T-seal to secure the mesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring it remains in place over the defect areas.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum primarily using a two-hole D-lock absorbable suture in a running fashion, ensuring the closure is secure and continuous.
